responses.strategic_rationale: The plan confronts core tensions between rigorous scientific data capture and humane, naturalistic participant environments; high-resolution, multi-stream sensing versus privacy and cost; tight ethics/compliance versus scalable enrollment. Five levers balance environment, governance, protocol flexibility, recruitment, and sensing-analytics architecture to enable safe expansion, cross-site benchmarking, and measurable methodological validation. Emphasis on privacy-by-design data governance and modular sensing supports longitudinal phenotyping while constraining risk. Radical options push innovative governance, adaptive protocols, and federated data practices to accelerate learning without compromising participant safety or data privacy. Trade-offs are explicit: safety vs naturalism, privacy vs utility, flexibility vs reproducibility, speed vs representativeness, and data richness vs cost. The levers collectively mitigate pilot risk and enable evidence-based expansion or withdrawal decisions.

levers.name: Residential Operations & Environment Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Safety baseline preserved and domestic ambience maintained → Systemic: Throughput rises 25% via modular room use and dynamic staffing → Strategic: Enables scalable, privacy-conscious expansion and external collaborations; Trade-offs: Safety vs Naturalistic Behavior; Privacy vs Throughput
options: Preserve a naturalistic residential setting with standard safety features and fixed night staffing to maximize welfare and measurement fidelity.
Enable modular safety upgrades and flexible staffing to lift throughput while maintaining welfare and data quality.
Adopt a privacy-preserving, modular environment with edge-computing triage and external partnerships to scale to 12-16 suites within 18 months.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Controls Safety vs Naturalistic Behavior. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term maintenance costs and staffing burnout.

levers.name: Data Governance & Privacy Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Consent and de-identification controls tightened → Systemic: Cross-site data validation improves by 30% via controlled licensing for data sharing → Strategic: Enables broader benchmarking while preserving privacy; Trade-offs: Privacy vs Data Utility; Regulatory compliance vs speed
options: Maintain local data stewardship with strict de-identification and offline storage, limiting external access to sustain risk posture.
Adopt standardized governance with de-identified data exchange under formal data-use agreements to enable external validation without compromising privacy.
Establish a privacy-preserving analytics framework using federated processing across partner labs and a cross-institution data-sharing consortium with differential privacy and controlled licensing.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Privacy vs Data Utility. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-border regulatory variability and a concrete licensing framework.

levers.name: Adaptive Clinical-Research Protocol Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Interim analyses enable safe protocol adjustments → Systemic: Data yield per participant increases by 20% due to scheduled night-pattern adaptations → Strategic: Accelerates method validation and publication cadence; Trade-offs: Flexibility vs Reproducibility
options: Maintain a fixed pre-admission protocol with rigid screening and limited interim changes to protect data integrity.
Incorporate planned adaptive elements with milestone-based modifications to Night Schedule and observation windows under pre-specified criteria.
Implement a modular, data-driven protocol with real-time decision rules and cross-site validation, complemented by a consortium-based method optimization for shared learning.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Flexibility vs Reproducibility. Weakness: The options fail to define risk management for protocol changes and safety oversight during adaptive changes.

levers.name: Participant Recruitment & Engagement Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Expanded outreach increases eligibility screening and consent rates → Systemic: Recruitment throughput improves by 40% via targeted channels and partner referrals → Strategic: Accelerates enrollment to meet sample targets while preserving representativeness; Trade-offs: Speed vs Representativeness
options: Rely on established channels (clinic referrals, local advertisements) with standard consent processes and fixed compensation.
Broaden recruitment through regional neurologists, patient groups, and enhanced compensation aligned with stay length to boost participation.
Create a regional participant consortium funded by sponsor-backed participation credits and hybrid remote pre-screening with on-site verification to rapidly scale enrollment.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Speed vs Representativeness. Weakness: The options fail to consider consent fatigue and equitable access across demographic groups.

levers.name: Sensing Infrastructure & Analytics Architecture Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Current sensing stack remains compliant with privacy constraints → Systemic: Data richness and annotation throughput increase by 30% through higher fidelity nights and streamlined scoring → Strategic: Enables robust event-triage model validation and cross-lab reuse; Trade-offs: data richness vs privacy; cost vs quality
options: Maintain existing tiered sensing with standard data handling, ensuring privacy constraints and offline scoring.
Upgrade to higher fidelity nocturnal measurements with more participants, improved synchronization, and optimized annotation throughput.
Adopt a modular, privacy-preserving analytics stack with edge-enabled processing and federated triage scoring across partner sites, plus a cross-lab data-annotation consortium for licenses.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Data Richness vs Privacy. Weakness: The options fail to consider vendor lock-in and interoperability constraints within the host IT infrastructure.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is cross-institutional risk governance and data-sharing governance beyond Bonn. Add 'Cross-institution privacy-by-design governance with federated de-identification, differential privacy, and a controlled data-access licensing framework' to Data Governance & Privacy Strategy.

responses.strategic_rationale: The Bonn parasomnia residential plan operates under three core tensions: ensuring rigorous, high-quality data in a safe, controlled environment; delivering timely insights within a demanding clinical-research budget; and balancing open scientific impact with participant privacy. The five levers below address facility design and governance, sensing and data architecture, recruitment/flow management, governance/publication cadence, and funding/sustainability. The progression from conservative to radical options enables staged risk-taking, clarifies dependencies (ethics approvals, staffing, computing capacity), and calibrates external collaboration. Together they support scalable methods validation, robust longitudinal phenotyping, and mature event-triage capabilities while preserving safety, ethics, and scientific credibility.

levers.name: Adaptive Facility and Governance Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Safety and privacy protections remain robust while enabling controlled testing of expansion; Systemic: 25% faster scaling through modular space design and standardized operating procedures, reducing onboarding time for new staff and sites; Strategic: enables rapid replication to additional cohorts or sites with higher upfront capital and tighter governance. Trade-offs: Safety/privacy controls slow expansion and add oversight complexity.
options: Maintain 8 active suites with enhanced safety and preserve domestic ambience, deferring major renovations and data-system upgrades until predefined safety and data-quality KPIs are achieved.
Expand to 12 modular suites with flexible staffing, deploy centralized data triage software, and implement milestone-based budget injections tied to enrollment and event capture KPIs.
Reconfigure into a modular, privacy-preserving, federated-analytics campus with cross-institution collaboration and outcome-based partnerships to fund expansion as KPIs are met.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Safety and Privacy vs. Operational Flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to anticipate regulatory delays or staff burnout that slow scaling.

levers.name: Sensing and Data Architecture Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Upgrades to data flow and sensing capabilities improve capture fidelity; Systemic: 25% faster scaling through standardized data formats and reliable on-prem pipelines, enabling faster onboarding of new technicians; Strategic: unlocks cross-site analytics and richer phenotyping, but increases capital expenditure and privacy governance complexity. Trade-offs: data richness vs. privacy/cost.
options: Maintain current dry-electrode EEG and mattress sensors, on-prem storage, encrypted backups, and restrict data streams to essential channels; avoid cloud processing.
Introduce hybrid sensing with cloud-backed processing, standardize data formats (EEG-BIDS), implement event tagging pipeline, and moderate sampling rates.

Deploy a fully integrated sensing fabric with on-device feature extraction and privacy-preserving federated analytics; establish cross-institution de-identified data lake and a new funding model to support expansion.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Data depth vs. privacy and cloud-cost exposure. Weakness: Options insufficiently address cross-site data governance and vendor lock-in risks.

levers.name: Recruitment and Study Flow Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Recruitment efficiency improves with broader reach; Systemic: 25% faster ramp-up of enrolled participants through diversified channels and optimized scheduling; Strategic: increases statistical power and generalizability, but raises regulatory scrutiny and variability in data quality. Trade-offs: speed vs. representativeness.
options: Maintain current recruitment channels, per-night compensation of €80, and keep stay cap; do not alter inclusion criteria.
Broaden recruitment channels to regional clinics; implement retention bonuses; restructure to enroll 50–70 participants; adjust inclusion criteria.
Decentralized registry with remote pre-screening and home-based intermittent monitoring; outcome-based reimbursement; cross-site expansion.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Recruitment speed vs. sample representativeness. Weakness: Options do not fully address potential misalignment between self-reported episodes and clinically validated events.

levers.name: Data Governance, Ethics, and Publication Cadence Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Privacy protections remain stringent while enabling more timely data interpretation; Systemic: 25% faster scaling through streamlined approvals and reproducible pipelines, reducing lead times for publications; Strategic: enhances external credibility and reproducibility, but elevates governance overhead and potential limits on real-time collaboration. Trade-offs: Privacy protections vs openness.
options: Continue with current de-identification and restricted video constraints; publish after data capture.
Implement expanded de-identification pipelines, external data-use agreements, quarterly outputs, and faster peer review.
Establish an open data commons with cryptographic access controls and standardized, federated scoring protocol for external researchers.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Privacy protections vs openness. Weakness: Options underplay data curation and reproducibility across external researchers.

levers.name: Partnerships and Sustainability Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Diversified funding reduces single-source risk; Systemic: 25% faster scaling through staged, milestone-based investments and shared resources, enabling broader study scope; Strategic: accelerates long-term impact and multi-site collaboration, but increases governance complexity and potential misalignment of incentives. Trade-offs: funding stability vs independence.
options: Pursue existing grants (DFG, University of Bonn) and internal funding; maintain current cost envelope; no pharma or external services.
Pursue additional grants and industry partnerships with defined rights; share publications; scale participant numbers.
Adopt a venture-like, milestone-based multi-funder funding model; offer external research services with outcome-based payments; foster cross-sector collaboration.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Funding stability vs independence. Weakness: Options may undervalue long-term sustainability beyond three years.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is cross-site data governance and reproducible analytics pipelines that would enable external validation while preserving privacy. Add 'Open data commons with cryptographic access controls and standardized, federated scoring protocol for external researchers' to Data Governance, Ethics, and Publication Cadence Strategy.

responses.strategic_rationale: The core tensions in this Bonn parasomnia plan revolve around speed of capability build versus data quality, privacy versus openness, and containment of cost against the need for rigorous, longitudinal phenotyping. Five levers address architecture (monitoring model), governance (privacy/data sharing), collaboration (industry-academic alignment), recruitment (participant pipelines), and staffing/tech modernization. Progression follows conservative to radical, balancing immediate safety with scalable, auditable methods and external validation. Together they create a pathway for robust methods validation, reproducible phenotyping, and controlled data sharing, while maintaining ethical safeguards, regulatory compliance, and credible publication timelines throughout the 3-year horizon.

levers.name: Residential Monitoring Architecture
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deploy modular sensing with standardized interfaces → Systemic: 25% faster scaling through reusable data pipelines and cross-site replication → Strategic: improves scalability but increases upfront capital risk and governance complexity significantly.
options: Maintain eight-suite core and intensify on-site scoring with safety buffers, deferring expansion until data quality metrics meet predefined thresholds.
Scale to twelve–sixteen suites with automation-assisted annotation and real-time flagging, preserving two independent raters for adjudication.
Adopt edge-to-cloud federated analytics and a subscription-based triage service to enable remote collaboration and multi-site data sharing under privacy constraints.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Controls Speed vs. Data Quality. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term patient privacy risk and regulatory complexity.

levers.name: Data Governance and Privacy Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Centralize de-identification and enforce strict access controls; Systemic: 25% faster data curation through standardized privacy filters and audit trails; Strategic: reduces leakage risk but may constrain collaboration and replication.
options: Preserve strict de-identification and local storage with no beyond-institution data access.
Institute tiered data sharing with controlled access via data use agreements and a governance board.
Launch a data marketplace with centralized governance and de-identified datasets accessible under a trusted access framework.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Privacy Centralization vs. Collaboration. Weakness: The options fail to consider external regulatory audits and cross-institution governance challenges.

levers.name: Academic-Industry Collaboration Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Align funding with scientific milestones and preserve publication independence; Systemic: 25% faster knowledge transfer via shared resources and synchronized reporting; Strategic: accelerates translation but risks diluted verification and control.
options: Maintain traditional research-only collaborations with milestone-based funding and standard publication rights.
Establish staged co-development with pharmaceutical partners on non-diagnostic metrics; shared authorship and milestone-revenue sharing.
Launch a cross-sector collaboration framework with co-ownership of results, joint governance, and licensing for downstream event-triage tools.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Publication Velocity vs. Independence. Weakness: The options fail to consider conflicts of interest and opaque governance risk.

levers.name: Recruitment and Retention Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Expand outreach and provide travel support; Systemic: 25% improvement in enrollment efficiency via dynamic screening and retention protocols; Strategic: improves representativeness but increases logistical complexity and budget volatility significantly.
options: Maintain tight pre-screening with high per-night compensation and strict inclusion criteria, preserving safety and data quality.
Engage community partnerships, flexible scheduling, and robust retention protocols to broaden participation and minimize attrition.
Implement rolling enrollment with dynamic inclusion criteria, flexible travel support, and remote pre-screening to maximize reach while preserving safety.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Reach vs. Safety. Weakness: The options fail to consider regional participant burden and potential selection bias from travel requirements.

levers.name: Technological Change and Staffing Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Deploy cross-training and remote dashboards to reduce on-site burden; Systemic: 25% reduction in night-shift fatigue via automated triage and remote oversight; Strategic: increases staffing flexibility but challenges continuity long-term.
options: Maintain the current staffing model with 12-hour night shifts, on-site technicians, and safety margins, keeping expansion contingent on pilot data.
Cross-train staff for multiple roles, invest in remote dashboards, and implement automated quality control to reduce manual workload.
Deploy autonomous sensor maintenance and machine-learning-assisted remote monitoring with a modular, contract-based staffing platform leveraging global experts.
levers.review_lever: Trade-off: Autonomy vs. Continuity. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory oversight and data integrity risks with autonomous operations.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is regulatory agility and external ethics oversight across multi-site operations, including rapid adaptation to evolving guidelines and independent audits. Add 'Open data marketplace with multi-site governance and external regulatory audits' to Data Governance and Privacy Strategy.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate EXACTLY 5 levers per response. Do not generate more or fewer than 5 levers.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences MUST:
     • Chain three SPECIFIC effects: "Immediate: [effect] → Systemic: [impact] → Strategic: [implication]"
     • Include measurable outcomes: "Systemic: 25% faster scaling through..."
     • Explicitly describe trade-offs between core tensions
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional/innovative approach
     • Show clear progression: conservative → moderate → radical
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name levers as strategic concepts (e.g., "Material Adaptation Strategy")
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     • State the trade-off explicitly: "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]."
     • Identify a specific weakness: "Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences
   - NO "[specific innovative option]" placeholders
   - NO value sets without clear strategic progression

6. **Option Structure Enforcement**
   - Radical option must include emerging tech/business model
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
